steps.title: Test the carbon dioxide cue
steps.content: Marquez and Rao concluded that carbon dioxide does not serve as an additional cue for frog-biting midges. If that conclusion is right, adding carbon dioxide to frog calls should not make a trap more attractive than calls alone.

steps.title: Reject carbon dioxide support
steps.content:
• Choice A: More midges in the carbon dioxide trap would support carbon dioxide as a cue.
  • Choice B: Entering only during or after carbon dioxide release suggests midges respond to carbon dioxide.
  • Choice D: Greater attraction at higher carbon dioxide levels would directly contradict the conclusion.
The answer is C.
